title: SEGMENT 6 — VRE: vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus and the D-Ala-D-Lac switch

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — VRE: vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus and the D-Ala-D-Lac switch

The enterococci — Enterococcus faecalis and Enterococcus faecium, mainly — are gut commensals that occasionally cause disease, particularly bloodstream infections, urinary tract infections, and endocarditis.
They are notoriously hardy.
They tolerate bile salts.
They survive on hospital surfaces for weeks.
They are intrinsically resistant to many antibiotics that work on other gram-positives, including most cephalosporins.
Vancomycin, when it was introduced in the 1950s, worked against them — until it didn't.

Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus — VRE — emerged in the late 1980s, first in Europe and then in the US, and within a decade became one of the major causes of healthcare-associated infections in hospitalized patients, particularly those in oncology and transplant wards on prolonged antibiotic regimens.
The mechanism is one of the most elegant — and frightening — examples of target modification in all of microbiology, so I want to walk you through it in detail.
Recall how vancomycin works.
Vancomycin is a large glycopeptide that recognizes and binds the D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of the peptidoglycan precursor that gets shuttled out to the growing cell wall.
By binding D-Ala-D-Ala, vancomycin physically blocks the transpeptidase from grabbing it for cross-linking.
The drug doesn't touch the enzyme; it covers up the substrate.
Five hydrogen bonds between the drug and the D-Ala-D-Ala dipeptide hold the complex together.
The cell tries to build a wall, vancomycin caps the precursors, the wall fails, the cell lyses.
It is a beautiful mechanism precisely because the target — a substrate, not an enzyme — was thought to be unmutable.
You can mutate a protein; you can't really mutate a dipeptide.
Except the enterococci figured out how to mutate the dipeptide.
The trick is breathtaking.
Instead of changing the D-Ala-D-Ala, the bacterium switches to making a different dipeptide entirely: D-Ala-D-Lac.
D-alanine-D-lactate.
Not D-Ala-D-Ala.
The last amino acid has been replaced with a lactate — a hydroxyl group instead of an amino group at that position.
The change is tiny: one oxygen replaces one nitrogen.
But it removes one of the five hydrogen bonds that hold vancomycin to its substrate, and that single missing hydrogen bond reduces vancomycin's binding affinity by approximately 1000-fold.
The drug can no longer effectively cap the precursor.
The transpeptidase grabs the D-Ala-D-Lac terminus (the enzyme is, fortunately for the bug, still happy to work with it), cross-links the wall, and the cell goes on building.
How does the bacterium pull this off?
It is not a single point mutation.
It is a coordinated transcriptional program involving an operon of about five genes — the vanA operon is the most common, with vanB and vanD and a few others as variations on the theme.

The operon includes: a sensor kinase, VanS, that detects vancomycin in the environment; a response regulator, VanR, that activates the downstream genes; VanH, which produces D-lactate from pyruvate; VanA (or VanB or VanD), a ligase that joins D-Ala to D-Lac instead of to D-Ala; VanX, a peptidase that cleaves any pre-existing D-Ala-D-Ala precursors so they don't compete; and sometimes VanY, which cleans up D-Ala from precursors that escaped VanX.
The whole apparatus is a coordinated molecular re-engineering of the cell wall precursor biosynthesis pathway.

And here is the killer detail: the vanA operon is on a transposon — Tn1546 — which often resides on a conjugative plasmid, which means the entire vancomycin resistance machinery can be transferred from one Enterococcus cell to another by direct cell-to-cell contact.
And, as we just saw in the VRSA segment, it can occasionally transfer to Staph aureus, completely across the genus line.
The whole modular resistance cassette — five or six genes encoding an alternative biosynthesis pathway — packs up, moves between cells, and confers full vancomycin resistance the day after transfer.
There is no laborious mutational evolution.
The recipient cell wakes up the next morning with a brand-new biochemical pathway.
VRE epidemiology.
By the late 1990s, VRE was endemic in many US hospitals, particularly E. faecium.
The proportions of vancomycin resistance among hospital E. faecium isolates in the US runs about 60-80% in recent years.

The clinical impact is significant — bloodstream VRE infections have mortality rates of 25-40%, partly because vancomycin is gone as a treatment option and partly because the patients who get VRE are already very sick.
Treatment options include linezolid, daptomycin, tigecycline, and a newer drug called oritavancin.
The pipeline is thin.

The VRE story also matters because it is the cleanest example of target switching, not target modification — instead of changing a protein to bind the drug less well, the bacterium builds a completely different chemical structure.
It is the most architecturally radical resistance mechanism I'm going to describe to you in the entire lecture.

And the speed with which it propagated, on a single transposon riding on conjugative plasmids, is the cleanest demonstration of why horizontal gene transfer is the dominant force in modern resistance evolution.
